contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC Asia Jakarta Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Canonical Codeforces statement and constraints; the current page also confirms the official 2600 rating and official tags.

Official Jakarta jury problem analysis; proves the pairwise p xor q threshold, gives sorting by A_i xor (p & q) as a simultaneous witness order, reduces the threshold to the minimum adjacent XOR after sorting A, and describes the binary-trie count.

Independent solution actually consulted; derives legality by high-to-low equivalence-class refinement, implements the resulting 0-1-trie path query, and separately confirms the simplified threshold t <= min_i(sorted_A_i xor sorted_A_{i+1}).

annotation.summary: Count index pairs 1 <= u < v <= M for which A can be permuted so that every earlier-later pair satisfies both cross-XOR inequalities with p = X_u and q = X_v.
annotation.constraints: 2 <= N,M <= 200000 and every A_i,X_i is a 30-bit nonnegative integer. Quadratic pair checks are impossible, and the answer can be as large as M(M-1)/2, so it needs 64-bit storage.
annotation.core_idea: For two values a,b, put d = p xor q and e = a xor b and compare d,e from the most significant bit. If the first unequal bit has d=1,e=0, then p and q differ there while a and b agree, so one required inequality has 1 on the left and 0 on the right under either orientation; the pair is impossible. If it has d=0,e=1, then p and q agree while a and b differ, and orienting the pair by increasing a xor (p & q) makes both inequalities true at that deciding bit. Higher bits were equal in both comparisons; if d=e exactly, both cross comparisons are equal. Hence a pair is orientable iff d <= e. For the whole array, necessity gives d <= K where K=min_{i<j}(A_i xor A_j), and sufficiency follows because sorting all elements by A_i xor (p & q) simultaneously chooses the valid orientation for every pair. After sorting A numerically, K is the minimum XOR of adjacent elements: if x<z<y, at the highest bit where x,y differ, either x xor z or z xor y loses that highest set bit and is smaller than x xor y. The answer is therefore the number of pairs X_u xor X_v <= K. Scan X left to right with a 30-level binary trie of prior values. While comparing x xor y with K, a 0-bit of K forces the equal-XOR branch; a 1-bit lets the XOR-0 branch contribute its whole subtree and continues through the XOR-1 branch. Include the final equality path, then insert x.
annotation.complexity: O(N log N + M B) time and O(M B) memory, where B=30.
tricks: Collapse the two cross-XOR inequalities for a pair to the single condition p xor q <= a xor b.
Use sorting by a xor (p & q) as one global witness order once every pair passes the threshold.
The minimum pairwise XOR is attained by adjacent values after ordinary numeric sorting.
Decompose x xor y <= K into complete binary-trie subtrees while scanning K from high bit to low bit.

solution_variants.name: min-xor-threshold-binary-trie
solution_variants.summary: Prove that (p,q)-xorderability is equivalent to p xor q <= K for the fixed threshold K=min_{i<j}(A_i xor A_j). Compute K from adjacent pairs of sorted A, then stream X through a binary trie and count prior y with x xor y <= K.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N log N + M B) time and O(M B) memory for B=30

tags.evidence: The structural proof reduces every requested pair exactly to the XOR-difference threshold X_u xor X_v <= K, so the remaining task is to count unordered index pairs by their XOR value under an upper bound.
A 30-level 0-1 trie stores prior X values with subtree sizes and answers each XOR-threshold counting query in O(30).

At every 1-bit of K, the branch producing XOR bit 0 is wholly valid and contributes an entire trie subtree, while the branch producing XOR bit 1 remains on the equality path; this is the decisive decomposition of x xor y <= K.
Sorting A makes the minimum pairwise XOR appear among adjacent values, and sorting by A_i xor (p & q) supplies the proof's global valid permutation.
